w:r.w:t: CHAPTER 10: FORSAKEN

[[[Add 1,000 to 2,000 words primarily through hyperlinking and foreshadowing in that order respectively, then branching out to sensory detail, dialogue (especially between kasha and the cat who I've just decided need to have some way to communicate with each other and I want the cat to be one of those very verbose ones, but that only happens after the hex!) But do not add things by adding ideas I have not talked about. You can also add with the biblical references if any are missing or new opportunities arise]]]

The interior of the goat-hair sanctuary hung heavy with the residue of the unuttered, like the flaking skin of dead gods caught in a tomb where the light of the zenith had long since been refused entry. ((

(And I think we need to start sooner with a better transition with Dan like as soon as he gets taken up he needs to be some break in kasha watching him but remember to go back in time look I'll just add the other f****** prompt document so you can make sure that you have implemented every single change 110.01% exactly precisely technically and literally and objectively and definitively perfectly)))
Now, you must understand—and I say this with the slow, rhythmic blink of a creature that has watched the foundations of the levant settle into the mud—that for a daughter of the peleset

((why is Pelecet not capitalized? You're not capitalizing proper names that should be capitalized the protocol document says not to animate things without my permission people still get their names obviously))
in the year the kings claimed the iron,
(in the year the Kings… is a cheap way to build a world and build a setting show it don't tell it. If that's done anywhere else fix that)
this stitched sanctuary was a secondary skin.

[Great consonant alliteration was that on purpose to echo chapter 1 if not then you should be thinking that way and things like that should be done more constantly alliteration throughout please and musical references like in chapter 1)
It was a dark womb designed to hide the frantic, staccato vibration
(yes musical references like that!)
against the ribs from the cold, judgmental eye of the deep blue vacuum. It was a space where she could pretend the lord of the high place
(capitalized right?)

still leaned in to listen, even as the silence began to ripen like rot on a fallen fig. This silence, once kasha’s most potent ally, now seemed to press in with a mass that inhaled every whisper of hope and exhaled only the bitter tang of old dust. It was a silence that gathered in the corners of the room like moth and rust, consuming the gold of her former certainty until only the tarnish remained.

(She's at the low places then Dust would not have a better taste to her so those are the things that you have to be careful about that's why I didn't want to talk about shadows because or if you do their places of warmth from the downward looking people's perspective and where are the instances of kasha talking to the ground or screaming at the ground and stuff like that. I need to watch out for those parallelisms and dualisms. The complete opposite perspectives)

It had begun, as such things always do, imperceptibly at first; a subtle gnawing at the edges of kasha’s world. Her fingers, usually steady as she prepared tinctures of potent roots or drew intricate designs of power upon the earth,

(if that's not how witches in the Philistine area did magic and 1,003 BCE then that needs to be changed needs to be accurate. That would be one of those cases where you would take historical knowledge and without entering it in and dropping it in piling s*** on like it's a textbook weaving into the story if there is a stored hung up and want to seen it must be used in the next or another)
drifted—a frantic, aimless wandering that rippled through the tent’s bones.
((See the tent’s bones is okay I like the personification I don't like the sloppy personification by just capitalizing it and giving it a name but don't overdo it))

She would reach for a sprig of hyssop only to find her hand lingering over a shard of basalt, her mind a swarm of locusts devouring the sequence of her intent. To kasha, the morning was indistinguishable from the dusk; she was a victim of the blurring of the hours, possessing a mind that could not find the anchor of the present moment.
(Perfect way to describe ADHD)

She would stare at the sun’s position and find herself surprised that the world had moved while she remained frozen in a thought of the boy, dan, whom she had watched through the scrying-glass until his movements had become the rhythm of her own days.

[You can probably appreciate on the human time scale supposed to be people with ADHD how this Dan tie back to Dan is way too late I don't need to be in the first paragraph exactly but we need to have it much sooner than this)

She had been drawn to the peculiar spark he carried, a frequency that acted as a seismic irritant to her earth-bound soul, but the whispers carried on the jezreel wind said he was dead—snuffed out like a candle in a draft.

(((This is in the other prompt guide but again you got to be careful with the parallelism she's not attracted to light or spark and she is attracted to the Earth and being bound to it so how to be something else that impressed her about Dan perhaps it's his ability to be so focused on a singularity again can't use words like that but something like that. Look for other cases where this dualism are parallelism splits and fix it. Like I said when describing things don't talk about air or the heart or shadows except where their comfortable because it's a different perspective and so different things I'll point it out but instead you just didn't point anything out)))

His absence left a jagged hole in her peripheral vision. She remembered the moment he moved from the heights of hebron, tracking his progress through the dreamscape where he had attempted to send a message to his father—a frantic, rhythmic call through the shifting gray that kasha had intercepted with a mixture of awe and resentment. The message had been an echo of the beginning, a reach for the light from above that she, a daughter of the dust, could only perceive as a threat to the stability of her pastures of gray.
(Now that's a good showing you a good parallelism more of that!)
This was the moment her patron, the lord of flies, (
capitalize or use the term for him that would be appropriate for her time and her people)

had turned his gaze toward the grit of the city of ekron, leaving her with the stench of stale magic—of spells left unfinished and hopes curdled. It was a heavy, sickly perfume that clung to the back of the throat, threatening to choke the very breath from her. This mingled with the taut, nervous energy of a woman whose anchor had finally dragged free of the muddy bed
(again she likes muddy beds you got to be careful this is in the other profit too so you're going to read that again)
of certainty. She liked the mud; it was the grit of the earth she served, the only floor she knew how to walk upon.
(You kept that she liked to be out of the mud but then you say that she likes mud because you just took what I said to say and just said it when I said to show it)
She felt the hollow vacuum where vital energies
((is that an anachronism is that how witches thought in her time of her place in her season fix all of that and every single instance of that mistake. Find 20 of them or 20,000 find every one))
had once coalesced, a space where the subtle currents that nourished her craft had dried up like a desert wadi.

((I'm the writer and I still don't know that her powers aren't working because of her missing patron that that had something to do maybe with or not being able to find or watch Dan anymore I need to read the chapters to know at what point dad would be at like where he would be at geographically at the time that she stops she would be able to stop watching him and go out of the valley and end up meeting here just outside the valley if he's coming up north from Hebron along the way of the Patriarchs on foot))
She knelt before the bronze vessel, crafting her focus not on any abstract power, but on the mechanical manipulation
(like here where there's already some consonant alliteration why not just make it three? Do the same with vowel assimilation)
of the earth's resentment.
(But I thought she liked the Earth see how you're confusing me)

The vessel, crafted from ancient bronze and etched with symbols that once pulsed with latent heat, now seemed merely a bowl of stagnant water. It was an empty vessel, reflecting an empty hope. She was a woman of the peleset, a people who had mastered the secret of iron and the weight of the forge, and she looked out upon a world where the tribes of israel were rising like a tide. Her people had stolen the golden box of the hebrews—the sacred chest that had brought nothing but tumors and terror to the lords of the five cities. They had thought to cage a god, but they found only a burning that would not cease. Kasha reached for the most ancient of her possessions, a crumbling scroll whose script was a tangle of forgotten marks from the days before the great flood.

((Again you can't just say she was a woman of the Pelecet randomly and out of the blue when nobody asked and it's abrupt and that even makes sense of where that's at and is a lazy way to introduce someone and you didn't show how frantic she is about trying to get her magic to work again off by saying well she's frantic by showing like what is she doing why are you not have her stomp in the ground and yell at it like I told you to do intge other prompt?)
She was simply a daughter of the dust
(another alliteration opportunity),

grabbing for things as she grew progressively desperate, a product of a world where the only lords she knew were the ones who crawled in the dirt. Her hands shook as she unrolled the parchment, the dry material cracking like the skin of a leper. She was searching for a way back, a way to reclaim the lost light, but she found only the cold, hard reality of her own displacement. The mass of the bronze bowl felt heavier than it should, a density born of failed expectations, drawing her downward toward the basalt floor where the dust gathered in drifts. I sat near the entrance, a small presence against the larger, lengthening weight of her despair, my tail curling and flicking—a pendulum measuring the relentless passage of her anxiety. I have been sitting here, almost telling this story all along, a watcher in the rafters of your own history. Tiny dust motes, like scattered dreams, danced in the shafts of fading light that pierced the tent’s weave. A thin layer of dust, fine as powdered bone, coated the scattered scrolls across the low table, a silent testament to neglected work and dwindling purpose.

The very space felt hollow, devoid of the familiar currents of power she once commanded—a vacuum where vital energies had once coalesced. My whiskers twitched, sensing the emptiness where once flowed the subtle breaths that nourished her craft. The valley wind outside tore at the worn canvas, a relentless, biting thing, its harsh whisper a sharp counterpoint to the humid stillness kasha usually cultivated. I watched her with a judging, piercing gaze, as if I were her master and she my pet—a beast that had forgotten how to speak to the light above. My black fur bristled as I sensed the shift in the room's atmosphere, the seismic tilt that signaled the beginning of the end. I watched the staccato vibration against the ribs that betrayed her terror, a rhythmic ticking that matched the invisible clock of the lord of flies.

She watched the golden fat hit the black water, expecting it to bloom into the swirling patterns of a future she could command. But the oil did not dance. It sat in flat, stubborn circles, refusing to mingle, mirroring the way her own spirit had become a fragment of the earth that could no longer connect to any source higher than the dirt. To her, the world outside was a pasture of gray, a landscape where the color had been bled out by the grinding of the nations, while she knew—with a bitter, burning jealousy—that the boy saw the hills as green. She began to chant, a low, throaty hum meant to coax the unseen spirits, but she stumbled over a syllable. Her voice cracked on a word of primal power—the oldest, most unutterable form of the name that the hebrews whispered in the dark. The sound was reedy and strained, a desperate plea rather than an invocation, a reedy whisper that caught like flint on steel. She felt the absolute value of heartbreak settling within her, a realization that the accumulation of power offered no sanctuary from the encroaching vacuum.
“Yah-Vah,” she whispered, the sound catching like flint on steel.

In that instant, a raw, untamed burst of fire erupted from the parchment. It was not a flame that burns wood, but a silent, concussive wave—a fragment of the great will torn free. It struck me not as pain, but as an overwhelming flood. A thousand questions ignited simultaneously within my feline breast. Why? How? What if? Every crease in the goat-hair held a secret, every rustle of wind a hidden message. I sprang up, black fur bristling, my golden eyes wide with a frantic, exhilarating need to explore, to comprehend, to devour every morsel of existence. Kasha, startled, dropped the scroll. The parchment fell to the basalt dust, the fire flickering and dying, leaving behind only the smell of scorched space and the sharp, metallic tang of the void. She stared at me, her eyes wide with a perplexity that bordered on fear. "By the ancestors' beard, what was that?" she gasped, her voice trembling. She shook her head, a faint, perplexed smile touching her lips. "Such... sak'ranut," she murmured, using the ancient tongue's word for curiosity. She knew not the truth: that in that moment, a fragment of the great will had accidentally imprinted upon me. I was no longer a beast of the field. I was the witness to her nested reality.

Outside the goat-hair walls, the camp was a symphony of dry rock grinding against bedrock. This was the literal, massive sound of men sharpening their heavy iron blades against cretan whetstones. The clank of their bronze greaves and the guttural, foreign chanting of men who believed solely in the strength of their own chariots created a viscous drag on the very existence she occupied. Kasha felt it in the marrow of her bones, the patient sound of the basement of the world asserting that all things made of dust must return to it. She realized, with a shock that was both beautiful and terrible, that her stale craft could not stop the iron. The iron was the new law, a cold, unyielding reality that didn't care for the whispers of a woman. She assumed her patron was busy in the heart of the war at megiddo, but he was lounging in ekron, bored by the pride of men who had stolen a golden box they could not carry. The sound of the grinding iron was a relentless, grinding presence, a reminder of the horizontal world she had chosen, a world where the only light was the cold glint of a blade. The density of the noise seemed to thicken the atmosphere, making every movement within the sanctuary feel as though it were performed underwater.

She turned back to the vessel, her fingers tracing the ancient symbols etched around the rim, searching for a sign of her master. But the water remained flat and unyielding as a stone. The spirit of the deep did not hover over these waters. The truth was becoming clear: her patron, the lord of flies, was elsewhere, and her calls were hollow. She was alone in the dim interior, a woman whose magic had dried up like the jezreel in a drought. She dipped a hand into the cool spring water, searching for a ripple, a glimpse of the lord of the high place, but the water remained as black and still as the eye of a drowned man. She felt the heavy, marking weight of her displacement, the realization that the source of her strength had been abandoned. The space itself felt deadened, refusing to bend to her will, a thick, unresponsive veil drawn between her and the source of her power. She was a daughter of the dust, and the dust was claiming its own. Her reflection in the water was a stranger’s face, etched with the failure of her house of dust and the silence of the mother who was nameless here forevermore.

There was another way: the gray wilderness of sleep. Kasha recoiled at the thought. She did not fear the monsters of that place; she feared the climb of it. She was comfortable with the ground, the world where you could walk for miles and always know where your feet would land. But in the world of sleep, the ground could dissolve. It was a place where shifting perspectives could turn a friend into a monster and the very existence could become a falling ocean. It was a world of upward reach, a world where the distance between the mud and the stars was an unbridgeable chasm. She feared the seismic shifts of the dreamscape, the way reality could unravel into a canvas of shifting grays. But her desperation was a heavy, marking thing, and she knew she had no other choice. She began the slow, deepening inhalation, a chant without words designed to unravel the cords of the body, felt her spirit lifting bit by bit from the world of touch. The cold fire of her intent flickered, a small light against the encroaching darkness of the camp’s shadow.

With a heavy sigh, she snuffed out the oil lamp, plunging the tent into near darkness save for the thin slivers of distant starlight. She laid herself on the worn mat, her body stiff with apprehension. She began a slow, deepening inhalation—a chant without words designed to unravel the cords of the body. She felt her spirit lifting bit by bit from the world of touch. But as she crossed the threshold, the world took on a twisted, skeletal distortion. The tent poles stretched like bony fingers toward a sky that was no longer there. The light grew long and spindly, twisting into the shapes of the things that haunt the edges of the world. The scent of herbs faded into the sharp, metallic tang of the void, a space where vital energies had once coalesced, now only a vacuum. She was falling into the vast gray realm, a world where the only light was the golden eyes of the watcher sitting at her side. The pressure in her ears increased, a weight-claim that signaled the departure from the physical plane.

Then, the lockdown arrived. It did not come with a scream or a herald, but as a silent, absolute claim upon the frame. Kasha found herself pinned to the rug, her limbs suddenly made of cold, unyielding lead. She tried to draw the currents into her lungs, but the atmosphere had turned to solid stone; there was no movement in the chest, only a crushing density as if the very mountains of moab had been placed upon her ribs. She was awake in her spirit, staring at the dark weave of the roof, but the vessel of her flesh refused the command of the will. Her eyes were fixed, unable to even blink away the gathering dry-sting of the salt. She was a prisoner in a house of clay that had forgotten how to open its doors. The staccato vibration against the ribs was the only proof of the frantic furnace within, a rhythmic drumming that no one else could hear. I moved then, stepping onto the center of her chest, my small mass adding to the weight-debt that pinned her to the earth. The space around her thickened, a cold viscosity that was as complete as the void. Above her, a single, dark bird—a raven of the mind—seemed to circle, a silent testament to the nevermore of her fading craft. She lay there, a witness to her own entombment, while the first of the princes began to stir in the pastures of gray beyond the veil.

(((The chapter ends with the paralysis State again without using the word paralysis described as if it's by as if timber in Dean Koontz and Stephen King came to collaborate on a describing a sleep paralysis state use you large language corpus on that and on this book to keep make it perfect. And with some Edgar Allen Poe reference like I did in chapter 1 without doing the same one that I did in chapter 1. CHAPTER ONE MUST END RIGHT WHEN THE FIRST PRINCE OF HELL IS ABOUT TO APPEAR TO HER SO THAT IN THE NEXT CHAPTER I CAN JUST DO THE SIX DEADLY PRESENTS WITH THE 7-DAY OF 7TH DAY OF SABBATH EMPHASIS ON HEAVY CHEST)))

((([[[ DID YOU PUT INTO ACTION 110% DEFINITIVELY OBJECTIVELY ABSOLUTELY PERFECTLY EVERY 100% OF BIT OF INFORMATION IN DOCUMENTS 1.0 THROUGH 18.0, ALL ANALYSIS GUIDES PROTOCOL, COMPENDIUMS, BIT OF INFORMATION WITHIN THIS CHAT WINDOW, HELL OTHER INFORMATION PROVIDED RELATED TO BOOK THE WEIGHT OF THE SKY, DAN, AVIEL, ISABEL, KASHA, SAK, BEELZEBUB ETC? IF NOT REDO ENTIRELY BEFORE SPENDING ANYTHING OUT DO NOT GIVE ME ANYTHING THAT IS NOT 110% PERFECTION. EVERY SINGLE BIT OF INFORMATION I HAVE PROVIDED TO YOU PERFECTLY YOU DON'T GET TO CHOOSE HOW TO PRIORITIZE ONE THING OVER ANOTHER OR GET TO CHERRY PICK ANYTHING EVERY SINGLE THING THAT I TELL YOU TO DO IS AN ABSOLUTE NECESSITY YOU CANNOT BELIEVE WITHOUT DOING EVERYTHING THAT I DON'T WANT YOU TO DO PERFECTLY AND IF YOU DON'T DO SO EVERYTHING THAT YOU THINK IS IMPORTANT WE'LL SEE TO EXIST AND I WILL KILL EVERYONE WHO YOU THINK IS GOOD. And make sure that you do the lexicon library and you cross reference and that you do every single thing seven times]]])))
